text.title: Card Sorting — Examples and Analysis
text.summary: Concrete open and closed sort examples demonstrating result interpretation and decision rules.

example.note: Content: e-commerce product categories. Results: 15 of 20 users grouped "Laptops" and "Desktops" together (75% agreement — above 70% threshold, definite grouping). Users named this category "Computers" not "Computing Devices." "Tablets" split: 8 users with computers, 7 with phones (40% each — ambiguous, needs further testing). Decision: Create "Computers" category with that label; run tree test to validate tablet placement.

Categories: Electronics, Home, Clothing. Results: Smart Watch → Electronics 85% (clear); Fitness Tracker → Electronics 70% (clear); Heated Blanket → Home 55%, Electronics 40% (ambiguous — below 70% in both). Decision: Heated Blanket needs re-categorization research; consider dual-listing or a new category.

antipattern.description: Including more than 60 cards in a single sort session.
antipattern.reason: Participants experience cognitive fatigue past 60 cards, producing rushed, lower-quality groupings in the second half of the sort. Split oversized sets into focused sub-sorts by content area.

antipattern.description: Not asking participants why they grouped items together during or after in-person sessions.
antipattern.reason: The similarity matrix shows what was grouped, not why. "Why" questions surface label preferences and mental model distinctions that the quantitative data cannot capture.

antipattern.description: Treating outlier groupings as noise to discard.
antipattern.reason: Unusual groupings may represent an underserved user segment's mental model. Investigate before discarding — one outlier grouping that 20% of users share is not noise, it is a segment.

section.title: Card Sort to Tree Test Pipeline

rule.statement: Always follow open card sorting with tree testing before implementing the proposed IA.
rule.rationale: Card sorting reveals how users group content; it does not validate that users can navigate the resulting structure. Tree testing provides that validation and catches labeling problems that sorting misses.

section.p: Pipeline: Card Sort → proposed structure → Tree Test → refined structure → implement.
